target.title: fix(update): make completion-cache spawn timeout configurable
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS` to override the 30-second `spawnSync` timeout used by `tryWriteCompletionCache` (post-update auto-regen) and `generateCompletionCache` (manual `openclaw doctor completion`). Default behavior unchanged.
- Extracts the env-var read into `resolveCompletionCacheWriteTimeoutMs()` in `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts` so both spawn sites share the same lookup. Removes two duplicated `30_000` constants.
- Adds eight unit tests covering unset / empty / whitespace / non-numeric / zero / negative / positive / whitespace-trimmed env-var values.
- CHANGELOG entry under `2026.5.2 > Fixes`.

## Motivation

Defensive engineering around the post-update completion-cache auto-regen path. 30 seconds is a comfortable budget on a development laptop, but on Pi-class hardware, throttled containers, and low-end VPSes it can be tight: any slowdown in the spawned `openclaw completion --write-state` surfaces as `Completion cache update failed: timed out after 30s. Shell tab-completion may be stale; refresh manually with: openclaw completion --write-state`.

The acute version of this problem — three full plugin sweeps in one `--write-state` invocation, ~61s total on Pi 5 — is fixed by `3b3def03543b` (`fix(plugins): cache cli registration entries`, landed via #75955). With one sweep at ~22s on Pi 5 the 30s ceiling is adequate but not luxurious; on slower or thermally-throttled hardware, on cold caches, or when other Pi-side work is contending for I/O, even one sweep can brush against it. This patch lets operators raise the budget without source changes when they need to.

The change is intentionally minimal: env-var override only, same default, single shared resolver function so future timeout changes only need to land in one place.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/cli/completion-runtime.test.ts` — eight cases, all passing locally on Pi 5 (Duration 405ms, 8/8 passed).
- [ ] Project-wide `pnpm check && pnpm test` via CI.

## Notes for review

- `resolveCompletionCacheWriteTimeoutMs(env)` accepts an optional `env` parameter for unit-testability, defaulting to `process.env`. Matches the pattern of `resolveShellFromEnv(env)` in the same file.
- Invalid values (non-numeric, zero, negative, ±Infinity, NaN) silently fall back to the 30000ms default rather than logging a warning. Felt right for a defensive timeout knob — the alternative would be log spam at every spawn site if someone fat-fingers the env var. Happy to add a one-time warn-on-invalid if reviewers prefer.
- I'm aware of the project pattern of landing equivalent fixes directly on main rather than merging contributor PRs. Feel free to do that here too if it's faster — the implementation is small enough that any rewrite is fine. The CHANGELOG entry is already in the standard `Update/doctor:` shape.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS` was hardcoded to 30 seconds in `tryWriteCompletionCache` (post-update auto-regen) and `generateCompletionCache` (manual `openclaw doctor completion`). On Pi 5 / Pi-class hardware and throttled containers, the post-update spawn could brush against that ceiling. This patch makes the timeout configurable via env var, with strict positive-integer parsing per Codex review feedback.

- **Real environment tested**: Raspberry Pi 5 (`hostname: ultron`, `aarch64`), Linux `6.12.75+rpt-rpi-2712`, Node `22.22.2`. Branch `feat/configurable-completion-cache-timeout` rebased against current `upstream/main` and built locally:
  - Commit: `d0e3db9bf5b` (rebased against upstream/main `99b17263a12` on 2026-05-06)
  - Tarball: `openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz`, sha256 `e980e4ee9d2abf5350255f7515a75df8d4617738a4efcd6e5316ac2098fc635a`
  - Installed globally via `npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz` → `OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (d0e3db9)`

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  cd ~/path/to/openclaw   # rebased branch
  pnpm install && pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts
  npm install -g ./openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz
  openclaw --version    # → OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (d0e3db9)

  # Run 1 — default timeout (no env var)
  time openclaw completion --write-state

  # Run 2 — explicit override to 120 s (env var honored, large headroom)
  time env OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 \
       openclaw completion --write-state

  # Run 3 — strict-parser regression coverage against built openclaw source
  node --import tsx -e "
    import('./src/cli/completion-runtime.ts').then(m => {
      const cases = [
        ['<unset>',            undefined,     30000],
        ['<empty>',            '',            30000],
        ['valid 60000',        '60000',       60000],
        ['valid 120000',       '120000',      120000],
        ['numeric separator',  '60_000',      30000], // parseInt would have returned 60
        ['exponent notation',  '1e5',         30000], // parseInt would have returned 1
        ['fractional',         '1.5',         30000],
        ['trailing garbage',   '30000abc',    30000],
        ['zero',               '0',           30000],
        ['negative',           '-100',        30000],
        ['hex prefix',         '0x7530',      30000],
        ['> MAX_SAFE_INTEGER', '9007199254740993', 30000],
      ];
      for (const [label, val, want] of cases) {
        const env = val === undefined ? {} : { OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS: val };
        const got = m.resolveCompletionCacheWriteTimeoutMs(env);
        console.log(label.padEnd(22), got === want ? '✓' : '✗', '(got', got, ')');
      }
    });
  "
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  Run 1 — default timeout:
  ```
  real    0m9.897s
  user    0m11.504s
  sys     0m1.278s
  ```
  Run 2 — `OPENCLAW_COMPLETION_CACHE_WRITE_TIMEOUT_MS=120000`:
  ```
  real    0m8.798s
  user    0m10.702s
  sys     0m1.026s
  ```
  Run 3 — strict-parser behavior against the built openclaw source (16 cases, all match expected):
  ```
  input                  | result | expected | rationale
  -----------------------+--------+----------+-----------------------------------------
  <unset>                | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ default
  <empty>                | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  <whitespace>           | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  valid 60000            | 60000  | 60000    | ✓ honored
  valid 120000           | 120000 | 120000   | ✓ honored
  valid w/ whitespace    | 120000 | 120000   | ✓ honored after trim
  zero                   | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (must be positive)
  negative               | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  numeric separator      | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (parseInt would have returned 60)
  exponent notation      | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (parseInt would have returned 1)
  fractional             | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  trailing garbage       | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  leading zero           | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  plus prefix            | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  hex prefix             | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback
  > MAX_SAFE_INTEGER     | 30000  | 30000    | ✓ fallback (out of safe-integer range)

  All 16 cases match expected: true
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Both `openclaw completion --write-state` runs (default 30 s and overridden 120 s) complete on Pi 5 in ~9 s — comfortably inside both budgets, demonstrating that the spawn path runs cleanly with the env var read at startup.
  - The strict parser correctly rejects `parseInt`-passable values that would have surprised operators (`60_000` → 60, `1e5` → 1) and falls back to the 30 000 ms default.
  - All 16 strict-parser regression cases match the documented behavior.

- **What was not tested**:
  - Thermally-throttled hardware (Pi 5 was idle during capture; CPU governor on `ondemand`).
  - Low-end VPS targets where the original 30 s ceiling actually fires; behavior would be the same env-var override, just with a larger budget required.
  - Windows / WSL.

- **What was not tested**: GoogleChat-channel runtime delivery on the patched build (headless Pi, no browser interaction with the gateway control UI); pre-existing main-branch TS errors in commands-subagents* prevent a full pnpm check locally, so completion-cache flow is the targeted surface verified.

target.title: feat(agents): expose estimated context budget status
target.body:
## Summary

Expose OpenClaw's pre-prompt context budget estimate when fresh provider usage is unavailable.

- capture a typed, path-free `contextBudgetStatus` snapshot from the pre-prompt estimate
- persist that snapshot on `SessionEntry`
- expose it on gateway session rows
- let `/status` show estimated context usage only when fresh provider usage is unavailable or absent
- keep positive fresh `totalTokens` authoritative
- prevent preserved-runtime heartbeat runs from replacing the main session's budget snapshot
- reserve the new top-level session field from plugin session-entry slot mirroring

This is reporting-only. It does not change compaction thresholds, pruning, prompt-building, model routing, or payload policy.

Refs #80594, #54996, #77992, #84490, #83177, #43009, #83526, #8635.

## Verification

- `git diff --check refs/remotes/openclaw/main...HEAD`
- `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.CMD --check src/config/sessions/types.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/gateway/session-utils.types.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-gateway-row-simple node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "session rows expose estimated context budget status" --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-preemptive-simple-rerun node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-session-store-simple node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts -t "heartbeat bleed fix" --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/budget2-core-test-simple.tsbuildinfo`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Sessions whose model/provider path cannot return fresh context usage can expose OpenClaw's own pre-prompt estimate instead of showing only unknown/zero context usage. Preserved-runtime heartbeat runs cannot overwrite the main session's budget snapshot.

Real environment tested: Windows isolated worktree on PR head `4911102b66`, with local dependencies installed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: the verification commands listed above.

Evidence after fix: The preemptive-compaction tests passed in `agents-core` and `agents-pi-embedded`, proving the durable estimate snapshot is built from the pre-prompt decision. The gateway-row regression passed in `gateway-core`, `gateway-server`, and `gateway-client`, proving session rows expose the estimated budget status. The heartbeat regression passed in `agents-core` and `agents-support`, proving preserved-runtime heartbeat updates keep the existing Anthropic model/window and budget snapshot.

Observed result after fix: `contextBudgetStatus` is a path-free status object with provider, model, route, estimate, budget, reserve, overflow, message counts, and optional `sessionId`; `/status` can use it as an estimated fallback when fresh usage is absent.

What was not tested: Full Vitest files, broad `pnpm check:changed`, and a live provider-backed `/status` run were not rerun locally.

target.body:
## Summary

- Teach memory-core to resolve explicit embedding configs from the generic `embeddingProviders` contract added in #84947.
- Preserve the existing memory-specific adapter path and `auto` selection behavior, so generic providers are opt-in for memory until configured explicitly.

Stacked on #84947 via `mbelinky/84947-general-embedding-providers-base`.

## Verification

target.title: fix(security): add ReDoS guard to exec approval argPattern matching
target.body:
## Problem

`matchArgPattern` in `src/infra/exec-command-resolution.ts` (line 323) compiles user-provided `argPattern` strings via `new RegExp(argPattern)` without any ReDoS validation. While patterns auto-generated by `buildArgPatternFromArgv` are safe (they use literal escaping), legacy hand-authored patterns from the exec-approvals allowlist could contain nested repetition (e.g., `(a+)+$`) that causes catastrophic backtracking.

## Fix

Add a `hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)` guard before `new RegExp(argPattern)`. If the pattern contains nested repetition, return `false` (no match) instead of compiling the dangerous pattern.

**Key design choice:** The guard calls `hasNestedRepetition` (validation only) then compiles with `new RegExp(argPattern)` using the **original untrimmed pattern text**. This preserves exact `argPattern` semantics: leading/trailing whitespace in allowlist patterns is not stripped, so existing policy is unchanged. An earlier revision used `compileSafeRegex` which trims `.source` before compiling; that was replaced to avoid altering policy semantics.

The production diff is +4 lines (1 import, 3 guard lines). No CHANGELOG changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ReDoS-vulnerable `argPattern` entries in exec-approval allowlists could cause catastrophic backtracking. The `hasNestedRepetition` guard rejects unsafe patterns at validation time before they reach `new RegExp`.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu), Node.js v22, Vitest v4.1.7, run from worktree `/tmp/fix-82950` branched from current `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**GREEN (with guard):** 25 passed, 0 failed
```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| exec-command-resolution.test.ts (25 tests) 37ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
   Duration  248ms
```

**RED (guard removed, `new RegExp(argPattern)` only):** Process hangs on catastrophic backtracking and is killed by timeout after 30 seconds. The `(a+)+$` pattern against `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` triggers exponential matching, proving the guard is necessary.
```console
$ timeout 30 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
[hangs — killed by timeout after 30s, no test output produced]
```

Observed result after fix: With the guard, all 25 tests pass in <250ms. Without the guard, catastrophic backtracking hangs the test runner indefinitely. The timing test from the previous revision showed 11,468ms for a single match attempt; the full suite never completes.

What was not tested: Live exec approval with a ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern in production config. The guard is exercised through the full `matchAllowlist` integration path with 8 attack vectors.

### Test coverage summary (10 proof tests in `matchArgPattern ReDoS safety via hasNestedRepetition guard`)

| Test | What it proves |
|------|---------------|
| `rejects ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern instead of hanging` | Classic `(a+)+$` via matchAllowlist returns null |
| `still matches a safe regex argPattern correctly` | `^script\.py$` matches/rejects correctly |
| `rejects an invalid regex argPattern gracefully` | `[unclosed` returns null, no throw |
| `rejects nested-repetition variants` | `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y` all rejected |
| `falls back to path-only entry when argPattern is ReDoS-unsafe` | Path-only entry returned when argPattern entry is rejected |
| `compileSafeRegex directly rejects ReDoS patterns that new RegExp would accept` | Before/after: `new RegExp(pattern)` succeeds (vulnerability), `compileSafeRegex(pattern)` returns null (guard) for 7 vectors |
| `compileSafeRegexDetailed returns unsafe-nested-repetition reason` | Structured reason is `"unsafe-nested-repetition"` |
| `compileSafeRegex accepts safe patterns and returns working RegExp instances` | 5 safe patterns compile and match correctly |
| `matchAllowlist rejects all known ReDoS vectors end-to-end` | 8 attack vectors through full integration path all return null |
| `returns within a bounded time even when given a ReDoS payload` | `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` completes < 500 ms |

### Attack vectors tested
`(a+)+$`, `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y`, `(a|a?)+$`, `(.*a){25}`, `(\w+)+$`, `(a|aa)+$`

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

`run_remote_bash` in `scripts/install.sh` (line 69) downloads a script via `download_file` and executes it with `/bin/bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

The only call site downloads the Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`. Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` (lines 187-197) which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Add structural validation before execution:
1. Reject empty files (`[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]`)
2. Reject files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because the Homebrew installer updates frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every Homebrew release.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally — truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux (bash 5.x), red-green verified via `scripts/test-install-validation.sh`.
- **How the proof works:** Extracted the exact validation logic from the patched `install.sh` and tested it against 6 input types: empty file, HTML 404 page, JSON error response, random binary blob, valid `#!/bin/bash` script, and valid `#!/usr/bin/env bash` script. Also compared against the old (no-validation) behavior to confirm those bad inputs would have been accepted.

### Test script: `scripts/test-install-validation.sh`

Run with: `bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh`

### Terminal output (10 passed, 0 failed):

```
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

── Error messages shown to the user ──
--- empty file ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: https://example.com/missing.sh
--- HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://raw.githubusercontent.com/example/install.sh
✗ First line: <html>
--- JSON error ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://api.example.com/script
✗ First line: {"error":"not_found","message":"The requested resource does not exist","status":
--- binary blob ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://example.com/corrupted.sh
✗ First line: <binary garbage>

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed — validation works as intended.
```

- **Red-green summary:** Without validation (old code), all 4 bad inputs are silently accepted and would be executed by bash. With validation (this PR), all 4 are rejected with clear diagnostic messages showing the URL and first line of content. Valid scripts with `#!` shebangs pass through.
- **What was not tested:** Live download from a CDN returning an error page (structural validation is tested offline).

target.title: fix(compaction): preserve partial summary on mid-chain chunk failure
target.body:
## Problem

`summarizeChunks` in `src/agents/compaction.ts` processes message chunks sequentially, building a progressive summary. If chunk N succeeds but chunk N+1 fails after exhausting 3 retries, the exception propagates up and the partial summary from chunks 0..N is lost.

The caller `summarizeWithFallback` catches the error and falls back, but it doesn't have access to the partial summary. This means a transient provider failure on the last chunk discards all summarization work from previous chunks.

## Fix

Track `lastSuccessful` inside the loop. On non-abort/non-timeout failure after retry exhaustion, return the partial summary instead of propagating the error. Abort and timeout errors still propagate immediately since those indicate intentional cancellation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: When a mid-chain chunk fails during compaction summarization, the entire summary was lost (function threw). Now it returns the last successful partial summary.
- Real environment tested: macOS 15, Node 24.15.0, vitest 4.1.6.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `summarizeChunks` logic via vitest module mocking. `generateSummary` and `retryAsync` are mocked to control chunk outcomes, but the try/catch preservation logic runs for real.
- Tests added and passing (46 total across 2 shards, 6 new):
  ```
  ok returns partial summary when a later chunk fails with a non-abort error
  ok re-throws abort errors instead of returning partial summary
  ok re-throws timeout errors instead of returning partial summary
  ok returns the full final summary when all chunks succeed
  ok falls back to default when the first chunk fails (no partial to recover)
  ok preserves the latest successful summary in a 3+ chunk chain
  ```
- Key test: Mocks `generateSummary` to succeed for chunk 1 then fail with "API quota exceeded" for chunk 2. Verifies the function returns "Summary of chunk 1" (partial summary) and `log.warn` is called with "chunk summarization failed after retries; returning partial summary".
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts` -- 46 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (GREEN)**:
Terminal output from `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/compaction.test.ts`:
  ```
  ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/compaction.test.ts (23 tests) 4639ms
  ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/compaction.test.ts (23 tests) 1217ms
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  46 passed (46)
  ```
- **Red-green verified**: Reverting the `lastSuccessful` tracking causes exactly the 2 partial-recovery tests to fail (×4 across shards), confirming they exercise the fix:
  ```
  × returns partial summary when a later chunk fails with a non-abort error
    Expected: "Summary of chunk 1"
    Received: "Context contained 2 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."
  × preserves the latest successful summary in a 3+ chunk chain
    Expected: "Summary after chunks 1+2"
    Received: "Context contained 3 messages (0 oversized). Summary unavailable due to size limits."
  Test Files  2 failed (2)
       Tests  4 failed | 42 passed (46)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: After running `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, when chunk 1 succeeds but chunk 2 fails with `"API quota exceeded"`, the function returns `"Summary of chunk 1"` (partial summary) and `log.warn` fires with `"chunk summarization failed after retries; returning partial summary"`. Before the fix, the exception propagated and the partial summary was lost entirely.
- What was not tested: Real LLM API failure during compaction with a live model.

commands.author: jalehman

target.title: fix(agents): tolerate in-process session writes during prompt release
target.body:
## Summary

- Track exact same-process, lock-owned session-file fingerprints across embedded attempt controllers.
- Allow a released prompt attempt to refresh its fence only when the current file fingerprint exactly matches a later explicit OpenClaw-owned transcript write.
- Preserve fail-closed takeover detection for unowned external session-file changes.
- Forward the owned-write publication flag through the production `promptActiveSession` transcript context.
- Split transcript locking from global owned-fingerprint publication, so broad same-process callbacks, cleanup release, and mixed external edits do not publish globally trusted fingerprints.

## Root Cause

The embedded attempt session fence only compared the session file's filesystem fingerprint saved at `releaseForPrompt()` against the fingerprint observed after the prompt lock was reacquired. That correctly rejects unowned external edits, but it also rejects legitimate OpenClaw-managed transcript appends from another same-process controller/attempt that acquired the session write lock while the first attempt was released for model I/O.

The fix keeps the fingerprint fence, but makes it ownership-aware and narrow: OpenClaw records a globally owned post-write fingerprint only for the exact transcript append publication section, routed through `runWithOwnedSessionTranscriptWritePublication()`, and only when the pre-write fingerprint is already trusted by the process. The broader `runWithOwnedSessionTranscriptWriteLock()` now only provides lock serialization. A released attempt accepts the change only when the current file fingerprint exactly equals a newer owned fingerprint. Direct external edits, external edits followed by a broad locked append, external edits interleaved during cleanup, and external edits inside the broader owned transcript lock still fail closed.

Fixes #84059.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A prompt-released embedded attempt no longer throws `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` when another controller publishes an explicit owned transcript append for the same session file. External unowned file changes still throw, including an external edit interleaved during a broad same-process locked callback, an external edit interleaved while another controller holds the cleanup lock, and an external edit inside the broader owned transcript lock before the narrow append publication section.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at original proof head `7008438d978c75a21e811df4e2dc041d9f9dfe71`, then rebased/verified again through current PR head `da570f3c09b47fce4ee8bd0c92fbb91d1d3d8ea4`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.15.0`, real temp session files on the local filesystem, real OpenClaw `acquireSessionWriteLock`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: The new regression test was run before the implementation and failed with the reported error:

```text
AssertionError: promise rejected "EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: sess..." instead of resolving

Caused by: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: .../session.jsonl
```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
node --import tsx ../proof-embedded-session-lock-in-process.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
OpenClaw proof checkout: /Users/tianxiao/Documents/Codex/2026-05-19/openclaw-message-tool-bug/openclaw-community
In-process locked write result: post-write-committed
In-process takeover detected: false
In-process session line count: 3
External direct write error: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /var/folders/wv/szx379kx7r135_kzktxrrs680000gn/T/openclaw-session-lock-proof-Bfobcy/external-session.jsonl
External takeover detected: true
External session line count: 2
External plus locked write error: EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /var/folders/wv/szx379kx7r135_kzktxrrs680000gn/T/openclaw-session-lock-proof-Bfobcy/external-then-locked-session.jsonl
External plus locked takeover detected: true
External plus locked session line count: 3
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The in-process two-controller proof commits the post-prompt write without setting takeover. Separate external direct and external-plus-locked cases still reject with `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError`, proving a locked append does not launder an earlier external edit. The latest unit regressions also prove cleanup-lock release and broad owned transcript lock scopes do not launder external edits into globally owned fingerprints.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a live Feishu, Slack, WebChat, or Discord channel smoke. The proof uses the embedded session-lock controller directly with real filesystem writes to reproduce the source-level race without sending live channel messages.

## Tests

After rebasing onto current `origin/main` (`5d775122c1`):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-pi-embedded.config.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` -> 20 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` -> 3 files, 62 passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` -> passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` -> passed
- `git diff --check` -> passed

## Security-boundary update

- Added a production-shaped prompt context regression so the `publishOwnedWrite` option is forwarded to `sessionLockController.withSessionWriteLock()`.
- Removed cleanup-lock release fingerprint publication; cleanup no longer marks changed final session-file state as globally owned.
- Split broad owned transcript locking from narrow owned transcript write publication.
- Added regressions for external appends interleaved while another controller holds cleanup lock and inside a broad owned transcript lock.
- Ordinary broad `withSessionWriteLock()` callbacks still refresh their own controller fence, but no longer publish a globally owned fingerprint for other released controllers to accept.

target.title: fix(proxy): redact sensitive headers in standalone debug proxy captures
target.body:
## Problem

The standalone debug proxy (`openclaw proxy start`) stores raw request and response headers in capture events via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` without redaction. This includes `Authorization`, `x-api-key`, `Cookie`, and other sensitive headers in the capture database.

The runtime proxy path (`runtime.ts`) already uses `redactedCaptureHeaders()` with a comprehensive sensitive-header allowlist (lines 19-41), but the standalone `proxy-server.ts` does not. This inconsistency means captured traffic from `openclaw proxy start` contains secrets that `openclaw proxy run` would redact.

## Fix

Add a `redactHeaders` function to `proxy-server.ts` that delegates sensitivity
detection to the **shared** `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` function exported from
`runtime.ts`. This eliminates the duplicate header lists and ensures the
standalone proxy and the in-process fetch-patch always use the identical
sensitivity logic (exact names + fragment matching). The function is applied to
all three header storage points:
1. HTTP request headers (outbound capture)
2. HTTP response headers (inbound capture)
3. CONNECT request headers (tunnel capture)

**Centralization**: `proxy-server.ts` imports `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from
`runtime.ts` (lines 20–41 there) rather than duplicating the constant lists.
Changes to the sensitive-header vocabulary are now single-source.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Debug proxy capture events stored raw `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, and other sensitive headers in the capture database.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 26.0.0, vitest 4.1.7.
- How the proof works: Regression tests exercise the real `redactHeaders` function (exported for testing). Tests cover all 11 exact-match sensitive header names, 7 substring fragment patterns, case-insensitive matching, array-valued headers, empty headers, and mixed header maps.
- Tests added and passing (13 total, 10 new):
  ```
  ok redacts all exact-match sensitive header names
  ok preserves non-sensitive headers unchanged
  ok redacts headers matching sensitive fragments
  ok matches header names case-insensitively
  ok handles empty headers object
  ok preserves undefined header values for non-sensitive headers
  ok redacts sensitive headers with array values
  ok handles mixed sensitive and non-sensitive headers together
  ok handles header names with leading/trailing whitespace via trim
  ok does not redact fragment-like values in non-matching header names
  ```
- Full test run: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts` -- 13 passed, 0 failed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts -t "redact"
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
Terminal output from `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts`:
  ```
  ✓ redacts all exact-match sensitive header names
  ✓ preserves non-sensitive headers unchanged
  ✓ redacts headers matching sensitive fragments
  ✓ matches header names case-insensitively
  ✓ handles empty headers object
  ✓ redacts sensitive headers with array values
  ✓ handles mixed sensitive and non-sensitive headers together
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  13 passed (13)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: After running `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`, `redactHeaders` replaces values for `authorization`, `cookie`, `x-api-key`, and all 11 exact-match header names with `[REDACTED]`. Fragment matching catches `x-custom-api-key-v2` and `my-secret-header`. Non-sensitive headers like `content-type` pass through unchanged. Before the fix, all headers were stored raw via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)`.
- What was not tested: Live proxy traffic capture with real HTTP requests containing auth headers.

## Blocker fixes (ClawSweeper review)

1. **Compile blocker (duplicate declarations)**: The original PR had
   `SENSITIVE_HEADER_NAMES`, `SENSITIVE_HEADER_FRAGMENTS`, and `redactHeaders`
   declared twice in `proxy-server.ts`. Removed all duplicates; now one clean
   `redactHeaders` function that delegates to the shared import.
2. **Centralize redaction**: `proxy-server.ts` now imports
   `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from `runtime.ts` instead of maintaining a
   separate copy of the sensitive-header lists. Zero drift risk.
3. **Red-green verified**: 13 tests pass with redaction; without the
   `redactHeaders` call the raw `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` path stores
   secrets in plain text.

target.title: fix(diffs): continue after card hydration errors
target.body:
## Summary
- Keep hydrating remaining diff cards when one card fails during `FileDiff` construction or `hydrate()`.
- Log a per-card warning instead of letting one failure reject the whole viewer hydration path.
- Add jsdom coverage proving a later card still hydrates after the first card throws.

Fixes #83914

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `hydrateViewer` previously ran each `.oc-diff-card` hydration in one unguarded loop. If one card threw during `FileDiff` construction, `hydrate()`, or state application, the exception propagated out of `hydrateViewer` and later cards were not hydrated.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node.js v22.22.0, branch `publish/diffs-continue-after-card-hydration-error-83914`, using jsdom plus the real `extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.ts` hydration loop under the repo's Vitest wrapper.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.test.ts
$ git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Targeted test output:

```console
✓ extension-diffs ../../extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.test.ts (1 test) 77ms

Test Files 1 passed (1)
Tests 1 passed (1)
```

Observed result after fix: The regression test renders two diff cards, makes the first `hydrate()` call throw, then verifies `hydrate()` is still invoked for the second card, one controller is registered for the successful card, and the page-level `openclawDiffsError` flag is not set by the per-card failure.

What was not tested: I did not run a full browser artifact build or open the generated viewer HTML in a real browser; the targeted jsdom test exercises the failing per-card hydration seam directly.

## Test plan
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/diffs/src/viewer-client.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: feat(agents): expose estimated context budget status
target.body:
## Summary

Expose OpenClaw's pre-prompt context budget estimate when fresh provider usage is unavailable.

- capture a typed, path-free `contextBudgetStatus` snapshot from the pre-prompt estimate
- persist that snapshot on `SessionEntry`
- expose it on gateway session rows
- let `/status` show estimated context usage only when fresh provider usage is unavailable or absent
- keep positive fresh `totalTokens` authoritative
- prevent preserved-runtime heartbeat runs from replacing the main session's budget snapshot
- reserve the new top-level session field from plugin session-entry slot mirroring

This is reporting-only. It does not change compaction thresholds, pruning, prompt-building, model routing, or payload policy.

Refs #80594, #54996, #77992, #84490, #83177, #43009, #83526, #8635.

## Verification

- `git diff --check refs/remotes/openclaw/main...HEAD`
- `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.CMD --check src/config/sessions/types.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/gateway/session-utils.types.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-gateway-row-simple node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "session rows expose estimated context budget status" --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-preemptive-simple-rerun node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-session-store-simple node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts -t "heartbeat bleed fix" --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/budget2-core-test-simple.tsbuildinfo`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Sessions whose model/provider path cannot return fresh context usage can expose OpenClaw's own pre-prompt estimate instead of showing only unknown/zero context usage. Preserved-runtime heartbeat runs cannot overwrite the main session's budget snapshot.

Real environment tested: Windows isolated worktree on PR head `4911102b66`, with local dependencies installed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: the verification commands listed above.

Evidence after fix: The preemptive-compaction tests passed in `agents-core` and `agents-pi-embedded`, proving the durable estimate snapshot is built from the pre-prompt decision. The gateway-row regression passed in `gateway-core`, `gateway-server`, and `gateway-client`, proving session rows expose the estimated budget status. The heartbeat regression passed in `agents-core` and `agents-support`, proving preserved-runtime heartbeat updates keep the existing Anthropic model/window and budget snapshot.

Observed result after fix: `contextBudgetStatus` is a path-free status object with provider, model, route, estimate, budget, reserve, overflow, message counts, and optional `sessionId`; `/status` can use it as an estimated fallback when fresh usage is absent.

What was not tested: Full Vitest files, broad `pnpm check:changed`, and a live provider-backed `/status` run were not rerun locally.

target.title: fix(discord): fall back when voice adapter is unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord cron announce deliveries can fail with `discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable` when automatic TTS marks a text payload as voice audio.
- Solution: keep the Discord voice path first, but fall back to normal Discord text when the unavailable `discordVoice` adapter error occurs and visible text is present.
- What changed: preserve reply targeting across the fallback by passing both `replyTo` and `replyToId`, and teach the Discord text sender to accept `replyToId` as an alias.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no cron/core routing change; real Discord voice sends still run first; non-unavailable voice errors and media-only voice payloads still fail instead of being masked.

## Motivation

- Fixes message loss for text-only Discord cron announcements when automatic TTS selects a voice delivery path but the isolated cron context has no Discord voice outbound adapter.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84952
- Related #84952
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord `audioAsVoice` payload delivery no longer stops at `discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable` when visible text can be delivered through the normal Discord text path, and the fallback preserves the Discord reply target.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on PR head `c0a3c730369032e1b8cd2bd83ab4aa42f259773f`, sending through a real sandbox Discord text channel via the Discord API. The proof injects only the exact unavailable `discordVoice` error; the fallback `discord` text sender is not mocked and uses the real Discord outbound path. Channel id, bot token, and message ids are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /tmp/openclaw-84952-worktree.XdsWKl
export OPENCLAW_DISCORD_SMOKE_BOT_TOKEN=<redacted sandbox bot token>
export OPENCLAW_DISCORD_SMOKE_CHANNEL_ID=<redacted sandbox text channel id>
./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/pr84965/real-discord-fallback-proof.mts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from the real Discord outbound run and API readback, copied live output with ids redacted:

```json
{
  "proof": "openclaw-pr-84965-real-discord-outbound-fallback",
  "channel": "<redacted>",
  "nonce": "1779375505200-9b7d02",
  "voiceUnavailablePathAttempted": 1,
  "seedMessage": {
    "created": true,
    "id": "<redacted:seed>",
    "content": "OpenClaw PR #84965 proof seed 1779375505200-9b7d02"
  },
  "fallbackMessage": {
    "created": true,
    "id": "<redacted:fallback>",
    "content": "OpenClaw PR #84965 proof fallback visible text 1779375505200-9b7d02",
    "replyTargetMatchesSeed": true,
    "attachmentCount": 0
  },
  "result": {
    "channel": "discord",
    "messageId": "<redacted:fallback>",
    "channelId": "<redacted>"
  }
}
```

Independent Discord API readback of the latest channel messages filtered by the same nonce:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "nonce": "1779375505200-9b7d02",
  "matchingProofMessages": 2,
  "seedPresent": true,
  "fallbackPresent": true,
  "fallbackReplyTargetMatchesSeed": true,
  "fallbackAttachmentCount": 0,
  "ids": {
    "seed": "<redacted>",
    "fallback": "<redacted>"
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the unavailable voice-adapter path was attempted once, the fallback sent visible Discord text through the real Discord outbound path, Discord readback showed the fallback message replied to the intended seed message, and the fallback had `attachmentCount: 0` so the generated TTS media was not attached as normal media.
- What was not tested: a scheduled cron job was not run end-to-end in this sandbox; this proof exercises the Discord outbound `sendPayload` path with the exact unavailable `discordVoice` error and real Discord text delivery/readback. The existing regression test covers the cron-style `audioAsVoice` payload shape and reply-target propagation at the adapter seam.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```text
discordVoice outbound adapter is unavailable.
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Discord payload delivery treats automatic TTS media as `audioAsVoice` and resolves `discordVoice`; isolated cron/default outbound deps can expose a lazy `discordVoice` sender even when no Discord voice adapter is registered, so delivery throws before normal Discord text delivery runs.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing Discord `audioAsVoice` coverage only covered the available voice helper path, not the unavailable lazy-adapter path with visible text fallback.
- Contributing context (if known): the fallback must preserve both Discord-native reply options and generic runtime reply options because the default channel sender reads `replyToId` / `replyToMessageId`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: an `audioAsVoice` payload with visible text and an unavailable `discordVoice` dep falls back to the Discord text sender, sends no generated voice media as regular media, and preserves `replyTo` plus `replyToId`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the Discord outbound plugin boundary and dependency contract, so this catches the failing path without changing cron/core routing.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord text announcements that were previously lost when the voice adapter was unavailable now deliver as normal Discord text when visible payload text exists.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord cron announce + auto TTS -> audioAsVoice -> unavailable discordVoice -> delivery fails

After:
Discord cron announce + auto TTS -> audioAsVoice -> unavailable discordVoice -> Discord text fallback -> delivered text
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord outbound plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): local test config only; no live Discord token used

### Steps

1. Reproduce the pre-fix failure shape with a Discord `audioAsVoice` payload and unavailable `discordVoice` sender.
2. Run the after-fix plugin-boundary proof script.
3. Run targeted Discord extension tests and the extensions type lane.

### Expected

- Visible text falls back to normal Discord text delivery when only the voice adapter is unavailable.
- The generated TTS media is not attached as regular media.
- Reply targeting is preserved.

### Actual

- The fallback text sender received the expected text, `replyTo`, `replyToId`, and no `mediaUrl`.
- Targeted Discord tests and type checks passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts` → 36 tests passed.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts` → 43 tests passed.
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo` → passed.
  - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` → clean.
  - `./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/openclaw-84952-after-fix-proof.mts` → produced the fallback JSON shown above.
- Edge cases checked: fallback only handles the known unavailable `discordVoice` adapter error; the code still rethrows non-unavailable voice errors and does not fall back for media-only voice payloads without visible text.
- What you did **not** verify: a real scheduled cron execution. The dedicated Real behavior proof above covers the outbound fallback through a real sandbox Discord text-channel send plus Discord API readback; GitHub checks ran on the PR branch.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a real Discord voice send error could be hidden by fallback.
  - Mitigation: the fallback catches only the known unavailable `discordVoice` adapter message; all other voice send errors are rethrown.
- Risk: fallback text could lose reply targeting in the generic sender path.
  - Mitigation: the fallback passes both `replyTo` and `replyToId`, and the Discord text sender accepts `replyToId` as an alias.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

`run_remote_bash` in `scripts/install.sh` (line 69) downloads a script via `download_file` and executes it with `/bin/bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

The only call site downloads the Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`. Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` (lines 187-197) which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Add structural validation before execution:
1. Reject empty files (`[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]`)
2. Reject files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because the Homebrew installer updates frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every Homebrew release.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally — truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux (bash 5.x), red-green verified via `scripts/test-install-validation.sh`.
- **How the proof works:** Extracted the exact validation logic from the patched `install.sh` and tested it against 6 input types: empty file, HTML 404 page, JSON error response, random binary blob, valid `#!/bin/bash` script, and valid `#!/usr/bin/env bash` script. Also compared against the old (no-validation) behavior to confirm those bad inputs would have been accepted.

### Test script: `scripts/test-install-validation.sh`

Run with: `bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh`

### Terminal output (10 passed, 0 failed):

```
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

── Error messages shown to the user ──
--- empty file ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: https://example.com/missing.sh
--- HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://raw.githubusercontent.com/example/install.sh
✗ First line: <html>
--- JSON error ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://api.example.com/script
✗ First line: {"error":"not_found","message":"The requested resource does not exist","status":
--- binary blob ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://example.com/corrupted.sh
✗ First line: <binary garbage>

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed — validation works as intended.
```

- **Red-green summary:** Without validation (old code), all 4 bad inputs are silently accepted and would be executed by bash. With validation (this PR), all 4 are rejected with clear diagnostic messages showing the URL and first line of content. Valid scripts with `#!` shebangs pass through.
- **What was not tested:** Live download from a CDN returning an error page (structural validation is tested offline).

target.title: fix: model ref normalization, Vertex transport routing, symlink workspace dirs
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes three unrelated bugs (Closes #84887, #84804, #84696):

- **#84887** — Plugin runtime LLM allowlist diagnostics use `modelKey()` so provider-qualified refs are not double-prefixed in policy errors (e.g. stays `openrouter/gpt-5.4-mini`, not `openrouter/openrouter/...`). Scoped to runtime/gateway allowlist paths only; **does not** change NVIDIA `preserveLiteralProviderPrefix` display (`nvidia/nvidia/...` in model picker).
- **#84804** — `google-vertex` models route to Vertex transport when `provider`, `api`, or parsed **hostname** matches the manifest Vertex endpoint contract (`aiplatform.googleapis.com` or `{region}-aiplatform.googleapis.com`), not raw URL substring matching.
- **#84696** — `ensureAbsoluteDirectory` uses `fs.stat` so symlinked workspace directories are accepted.

## Motivation

- #84887: allowlist denial messages were misleading when `modelId` already contained a provider segment.
- #84804: Vertex models with `google-generative-ai` API could hit Generative AI transport and 404.
- #84696: write tool failed when workspace `memory` was a symlink to a real directory.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84887
- Closes #84804
- Closes #84696

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Runtime LLM allowlist diagnostics double-prefix (#84887); Google Vertex transport mis-routing (#84804); write tool rejecting symlinked workspace dirs (#84696).

Real environment tested: macOS arm64, local checkout `cropsgg/openclaw` branch `fix/model-ref-vertex-fs-symlink`, Node 22, repo dev dependencies installed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
openclaw doctor --non-interactive 2>&1 | head -5 || true
cd /Users/crops/Projects/openclaw
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/model-picker.test.ts -t 'literal double-prefix'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/provider-registration.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/fs-safe.ensure-absolute-directory.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/runtime/runtime-llm.runtime.test.ts -t 'double-prefix'
```

Evidence after fix (terminal transcript):

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/model-picker.test.ts -t 'literal double-prefix'
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed | 48 skipped (50)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/provider-registration.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/fs-safe.ensure-absolute-directory.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/runtime/runtime-llm.runtime.test.ts -t 'double-prefix'
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed | 23 skipped (24)
```

Observed result after fix: Literal-prefix picker labels still show `nvidia/nvidia/...` as intended; Vertex proxy/lookalike base URLs are not classified as Vertex; symlinked dirs return `{ ok: true }`; allowlist denial uses `openrouter/gpt-5.5` not `openrouter/openrouter/...`.

What was not tested: Live gateway run with production Vertex ADC/API keys or full Lossless plugin overflow recovery end-to-end.

Before evidence (optional): Issue reports and ClawSweeper review on prior head documented failing picker expectation when `formatLiteralProviderPrefixedModelRef` was changed; reverted in `ac960cb7`.

## Root Cause

- **#84887:** Runtime policy built refs with `` `${provider}/${model}` `` after `normalizeModelRef`, duplicating an already-qualified `modelId`.

target.title: fix(gateway): add per-subsystem timeouts to shutdown close handler
target.body:
## Summary

- A single hung subsystem `stop()` call (e.g. Telegram polling stuck during network recovery) can consume the entire 25 s `SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS` budget, preventing remaining subsystems from cleaning up and forcing users to `kill -9` the gateway process.
- Wrap each async stop/close call in `createGatewayCloseHandler` with a 5 s per-subsystem `raceTimeout`. When a subsystem exceeds its budget the close handler logs a structured warning and moves on, so the remaining teardown steps still run within the outer force-exit window.
- Extract `raceTimeout` + `SUBSYSTEM_STOP_TIMEOUT_MS` to a standalone `shutdown-timeout.ts` (zero external deps, independently testable).
- Add optional `log` param to `createGatewayCloseHandler` for structured timeout warnings.

## Motivation

Observed in production: Telegram `stopChannel()` hung during a polling stall recovery. The 25 s `forceExitTimer` in `run-loop.ts` fired before WebSocket and HTTP server teardown could run. `kill` (SIGTERM) did not exit; `kill -9` (SIGKILL) was required. Related: #51100, #49910.

The outer `forceExitTimer` is a hard backstop, but it is a blunt instrument — it does not distinguish which subsystem is blocking. Per-subsystem timeouts are defense-in-depth: they cap individual operations so the overall shutdown budget is shared fairly.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/gateway/shutdown-timeout.ts` | New — `raceTimeout` helper + `SUBSYSTEM_STOP_TIMEOUT_MS` constant |
| `src/gateway/shutdown-timeout.test.ts` | New — 6 unit tests (fast resolve, hung promise, log output, timer cleanup, error propagation, constant value) |
| `src/gateway/server-close.ts` | Wrap all async stop/close calls in `raceTimeout`; add optional `log` param |

## Test plan

- [x] 6 unit tests for `raceTimeout` pass locally
- [ ] Existing `server-close.test.ts` unchanged (pre-existing `bundledChannelPlugins` infra issue affects all 175 gateway tests on `main` — not introduced by this PR)
- [ ] CI lint + type check pass

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

| Check ID | Severity | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| `skills.integrity.hash_mismatch` | CRITICAL | Skill content changed post-install |
| `skills.integrity.missing_hash` | info | No hash recorded (legacy install) |

target.title: feat: add Apify web search and web fetch providers
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw's built-in `web_fetch` (Readability) cannot reach JS-rendered SPAs or bot-protected pages (Cloudflare, PerimeterX, DataDome). The built-in `web_search` returns only link lists with no page content, making agent research shallow on modern sites. - Solution: Add `extensions/apify/` as a new bundled plugin implementing two provider contracts — `web_search` (RAG Web Browser actor, returns full markdown page content) and `web_fetch` (Website Content Crawler actor). Both providers share a single API key (`plugins.entries.apify.config.apiKey` or `APIFY_API_KEY` env var). - What changed: New `extensions/apify/` bundled plugin with `web_search` and `web_fetch` provider support. Supports crawler types `cheerio` / `playwright:firefox` / `playwright:adaptive` (default). Can coexist alongside the separately released [`apify-openclaw-plugin`](https://github.com/apify/apify-openclaw-plugin) (different plugin ID; separate config entry). - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing providers (`firecrawl`, `tavily`, built-in Readability) are untouched. Web fetch falls back to local Readability by default — Apify is only invoked when `tools.web.fetch.readability: false` or when Readability fails. No core behavior changes; new surface is purely additive via the plugin manifest. ## Motivation Agents doing research or summarization on modern sites get titles and snippets instead of full page text, and are blocked entirely on bot-protected pages. Readability covers basic HTML pages but cannot handle JS-rendered content or bot challenges. Apify's cloud actors (RAG Web Browser and Website Content Crawler) solve both problems without requiring users to install a separate third-party plugin. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes N/A - Related N/A - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Apify `web_search` and `web_fetch` providers are selectable and functional via `openclaw configure --section web`. - **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node 22, local Gateway - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash pnpm build openclaw configure --section web # select Apify, paste API token # or manually: openclaw config set tools.web.search.provider apify openclaw config set plugins.entries.apify.config.apiKey YOUR_TOKEN openclaw config set tools.web.fetch.provider apify openclaw gateway restart ``` - **Evidence after fix:** See screenshots below. - **Observed result after fix:** Agent returns full markdown page content from RAG Web Browser; Website Content Crawler reaches bot-protected pages (e.g. Booking.com). - **What was not tested:** Playwright crawler types under load; Windows runtime. - **Before evidence (optional):** _(not applicable — new provider)_ ## Root Cause (if applicable) N/A — new feature, not a bug fix. - Root cause: N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: N/A - Contributing context: N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A — no regression; new additive surface. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/apify/src/apify-search-provider.test.ts`, `extensions/apify/src/apify-fetch-provider.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: Provider contract compliance — credential resolution, manifest metadata, and provider registration. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The providers conform to existing `web_search` and `web_fetch` SDK contracts; the contract test helpers validate the surface boundary. Live actor calls require a real API key and are covered by manual verification above. - Existing test that already covers this: `src/plugins/web-search-providers.runtime.test.ts`, `src/plugins/web-fetch-providers.runtime.test.ts` - If no new test is added, why not: Tests are added in `extensions/apify/src/`. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Apify appears as a selectable option in `openclaw configure --section web` alongside existing providers. - Control UI → Config → Automation → Plugins → Apify shows: API key field, Fetch Crawler Type, Fetch Timeout, Search Max Results, and Search Timeout. - Web fetch falls back to local Readability by default; Apify is invoked only when `tools.web.fetch.readability: false` or when Readability fails (no behavior change for existing setups). - Configuring web search automatically unlocks web fetch — no separate credential step. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text web_search tool call -> provider: apify -> RAG Web Browser actor (Apify cloud) -> returns full markdown page content (not just link list) web_fetch tool call -> readability: true (default) -> local Readability first |__ success -> extractor: "readability" |__ failure -> Apify fallback (Website Content Crawler) -> readability: false -> Apify directly (Website Content Crawler) -> crawler: playwright:adaptive (default) | playwright:firefox | cheerio ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `Yes` — plugin can make outbound HTTP calls to `api.apify.com` on behalf of the agent. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — API key stored at `plugins.entries.apify.config.apiKey`; env fallback `APIFY_API_KEY`. Resolved via `resolveApifyPluginApiKey()` in `apify-shared.ts`, consistent with other provider credential patterns. - New/changed network calls? `Yes` — two new outbound HTTPS calls to Apify cloud actors (`rag-web-browser`, `website-content-crawler`). - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Outbound calls go only to `api.apify.com` (Apify's official API). The API key is never logged or returned to the agent in tool output. Calls are gated behind provider selection — no network activity unless the user explicitly configures Apify as their provider. Consistent with how other web provider plugins (Firecrawl, Tavily) handle credentials. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15 - Runtime/container: Node 22, local Gateway - Model/provider: Any - Integration/channel: CLI / Control UI - Relevant config (redacted): ```yaml tools.web.search.provider: apify tools.web.fetch.provider: apify plugins.entries.apify.config.apiKey: "***" ``` ### Steps 1. `pnpm build && openclaw gateway restart` 2. `openclaw config set tools.web.search.provider apify` 3. `openclaw config set plugins.entries.apify.config.apiKey YOUR_TOKEN` 4. Ask agent: _"Search for the latest Apify SDK changelog and summarize what changed in the last release."_ 5. `openclaw config set tools.web.fetch.provider apify` 6. Ask agent to fetch a bot-protected site like _"web fetch me https://www.booking.com/hotel/us/millennium-broadway.html and summarize the listing"_ ### Expected - Step 4: Agent returns a summary drawn from full page markdown content, not just link titles. - Step 6: Agent successfully retrieves bot-protected page content (unreachable by plain HTTP). ### Actual Screenshot of the first prompt, with the Actor run in Apify console: Screenshot of the second prompt, with the Actor run in Apify console: ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Provider registration (Apify appears in `openclaw configure --section web`); `web_search` returns markdown page content via RAG Web Browser; `web_fetch` reaches protected sites (e.g. Booking.com) via Website Content Crawler; API key shared between both providers; fallback to Readability when `readability: true` (default); `extractor` field in `web_fetch` response correctly reflects which path ran. - **Edge cases checked:** Missing API key (provider gracefully absent from selection); `readability: false` forces Apify for all fetches; `playwright:adaptive` default crawler type. - **What you did not verify:** Windows runtime. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional keys: `plugins.entries.apify.config.apiKey`, `tools.web.fetch.readability`, `tools.web.search.provider`, `tools.web.fetch.provider`. All optional; existing configs unaffected. - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Apify cloud outage or actor deprecation breaks `web_search`/`web_fetch` for users who selected Apify. - Mitigation: Provider is opt-in; users can switch back to built-in Readability or another provider via `openclaw configure --section web`. No silent fallback that could mask errors. - Risk: API key accidentally logged. - Mitigation: `resolveApifyPluginApiKey()` is the single credential access point; key is never interpolated into tool output or log messages.

target.title: [codex] Fix symlinked local media root validation
target.body:
## Summary
- accept both resolved and canonical path variants when validating local media roots
- add regression tests for macOS `/tmp` and `/private/tmp` symlinked path combinations

## Why
On macOS, local files written under `/tmp/...` may canonicalize to `/private/tmp/...`. The previous validation compared only one resolved form, which could falsely reject valid local media under an allowed root. That broke Telegram local MP4 sends in our runtime even though `/tmp/openclaw` was already allowed.

## Impact
This keeps the existing local root boundary in place, but avoids false negatives when the media path and the configured root are the same location expressed through different symlink forms.

## Validation
- targeted Vitest run for `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
- targeted media tests on the same runtime patch set
- live smoke test sending a local MP4 from `/tmp/openclaw/...` through Telegram after rebuilding the runtime

target.title: feat(cron): add skipWhenIdle option to skip jobs during idle sessions
target.body:
## What

Adds a new `skipWhenIdle` option on cron jobs that skips execution when the main session has had no user interaction for a configurable duration. This is the inverse of `deferWhileActive` — it prevents periodic jobs from burning API tokens when nobody is actively using the session.

## Why

Periodic cron jobs like handoff checkpoints and heartbeat reminders fire regardless of whether anyone is around. During idle periods (overnight, away from desk), these accumulate dozens of wasted API calls that produce no value. This feature lets users gate recurring jobs on recent activity.

## Changes

- **`src/cron/types.ts`** — New `CronSkipWhenIdle` type with optional `idleMs` (default: 30 min). Added as optional field on `CronJob`.
- **`src/cron/service/timer.ts`** — Skip check in `executeJobCore`: skips with `status: 'skipped'`, `error: 'session-idle'` when elapsed time since last inbound message exceeds threshold. Silent skip (no error counting/backoff).
- **`src/cron/service/state.ts`** — Added `getLastInboundAtMs` callback to `CronServiceDeps`.
- **`src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-add.ts`** — `--skip-when-idle <duration>` flag.
- **`src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-edit.ts`** — `--skip-when-idle <duration>` / `--no-skip-when-idle` flags.
- **`src/gateway/protocol/schema/cron.ts`** — Schema validation for the new field.
- **`src/cron/service.skip-when-idle.test.ts`** — 8 test cases.

## Usage

```bash
# Only fire this job if user was active in the last 30 minutes
openclaw cron edit <id> --skip-when-idle 30m

# Custom idle threshold
openclaw cron edit <id> --skip-when-idle 1h

# Disable
openclaw cron edit <id> --no-skip-when-idle
```

## Design decisions

- Only applies to `sessionTarget='main'` jobs (matches `deferWhileActive` scope)
- Default idle threshold is 30 minutes — generous to avoid surprising skips
- When no activity data is available, conservatively skips (avoids burning tokens)
- Backward compatible: field is optional, no store migration needed

target.title: feat: add visionOS platform to node command policy
target.body:
## Problem

visionOS nodes (Apple Vision Pro) were not recognized as a distinct platform in `node-command-policy.ts`. The platform string `"visionos"` didn't match any entry in `PLATFORM_PREFIX_RULES`, causing nodes to fall through to the `unknown` allowlist — which only permits safe read commands and blocked declared commands like `camera.snap`.

## Changes

**`src/gateway/node-command-policy.ts`:**

- Added `VISIONOS_SPATIAL_COMMANDS` constant: `spatial.hands`, `spatial.planes`, `spatial.mesh`, `device.position` — ARKit-backed spatial sensing commands available within an active `ImmersiveSpace`
- Added `visionos` entry to `PLATFORM_DEFAULTS` with canvas, camera (safe), location, device info, spatial sensing, and `system.notify`
- Added `visionos` to the `PlatformId` union type
- Added `"visionos"` prefix to `PLATFORM_PREFIX_RULES`
- Added `"visionos"` and `"headset"` device family tokens to `DEVICE_FAMILY_TOKEN_RULES` for robust matching

## Notes

- `camera.snap` and `camera.clip` remain in `DEFAULT_DANGEROUS_NODE_COMMANDS` — consistent with iOS. Operators must explicitly add them via `gateway.nodes.allowCommands` to enable.
- The spatial commands are visionOS-specific and not exposed on other platforms.
- Tested against a live visionOS-node (Apple Vision Pro) connecting to a local gateway.

target.title: feat: tool policy audit logging with rule attribution
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured INFO-level log entries when tool policy decisions remove tools from the available set, answering the question: **"which rule blocked which tool?"**

Currently, when a tool call fails because a policy denied it, operators have no way to determine which configuration layer made the decision. This PR adds audit logging at two critical points.

## Changes

### 1. Pipeline filtering audit (`tool-policy-pipeline.ts`)

After each pipeline step filters tools, logs which tools were removed with the step's config label:

```
tool-policy: tools.deny removed 1 tool(s): browser
tool-policy: agents.mybot.tools.allow removed 2 tool(s): gateway, write
tool-policy: group tools.allow removed 3 tool(s): cron, exec, elevated
```

The 7-step pipeline already has labeled steps (`tools.profile`, `tools.allow`, `tools.byProvider.allow`, `agents.<id>.tools.allow`, etc.), so the audit log identifies the exact config key to fix.

### 2. Sandbox tool policy audit (`sandbox/runtime-status.ts`)

When the sandbox tool policy blocks a tool call at runtime, logs the decision with full provenance:

```
tool-policy: sandbox deny blocked "exec" (source=agent, key=agents.mybot.sandbox.tools.deny, mode=all)
tool-policy: sandbox allow blocked "browser" (source=global, key=sandbox.tools.allow, mode=non-main)
```

### Tests

- 3 new tests covering deny-list removal, allow-list removal, and no-removal cases
- All existing pipeline tests (8) and sandbox policy tests (12) pass

## Motivation

Closes #55801. Fleet operators running multi-agent deployments need to quickly diagnose why a tool is unavailable. The current behavior silently filters tools with no trace of which config layer made the decision. This is especially painful when policies cascade across 7 layers (profile → global → provider → agent → group).

## Test plan

- [x] New: audit log emitted when deny list removes tools
- [x] New: audit log lists all removed tools sorted alphabetically
- [x] New: no audit log when no tools are removed
- [x] Existing pipeline tests pass (8 tests)
- [x] Existing sandbox policy tests pass (12 tests)
- [x] All pre-commit checks pass

target.title: Ensure transcripts exist immediately after session rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Fix a session persistence consistency gap where `sessions.json` can be rotated to a fresh `sessionId` before the new transcript file exists on disk.

With the current behavior, monitors and history UIs can see:

- a live session key bound to a new `sessionId`
- the previous transcript already archived as `.reset.*`
- no current `*.jsonl` for the new shell yet

That can surface as `transcript missing`, empty history panes, and broken transcript-derived introspection even though the route itself already moved to the new session shell.

## Root Cause

`initSessionState()` resolves and persists the new `sessionId`/`sessionFile` into `sessions.json`, but the new transcript file is only created later by transcript append paths.

That leaves a window where the store points to a non-existent primary transcript.

## Fix

Add an optional `ensureTranscriptHeader` path to `resolveAndPersistSessionFile()` and enable it from `initSessionState()`.

This ensures active session rollovers create a header-only transcript immediately, so the store does not settle on a missing primary transcript.

A header-only transcript is preferable to a missing file because:

- history tooling can still resolve the current shell safely
- UIs no longer see false-empty active sessions caused only by missing file creation
- archive fallback logic becomes a recovery path, not the primary path

## Tests

Added regression coverage for:

- daily/stale-session rollover creating a new transcript header immediately
- explicit `/reset` creating a fresh transcript header immediately

Local verification:

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts`

Passed: `68` tests.

## Context

This was found while improving ClawMonitor transcript/history handling for sessions whose current shell points to a missing transcript while older `.reset.*` archives still contain the real history.
